records.ety: [F. vogue a rowing, vogue, fashion, It. voga, fr. vogare to row, to sail; probably fr. OHG. wag<?/n to move, akin to E. way. Cf. Way.]

defs: The way or fashion of people at any particular time; temporary mode, custom, or practice; popular reception for the time; -- used now generally in the phrase in vogue.
quotes: One vogue, one vein, One air of thoughts usurps my brain.

Whatsoever its vogue may be, I still flatter myself that the parents of the growing generation will be satisfied with what <?/<?/ to be taught to their children in Westminster, in Eton, or in Winchester.
Use may revive the obsoletest words, And banish those that now are most in vogue.
